original_article:
BUENOS AIRES (Bloomberg) — Argentina's deepening economic crisis has sent shockwaves through the country's chemicals industry as hyperinflation and currency devaluation force manufacturers to halt production lines.

French chemicals group Arkema SA reported that its Argentine operations have been forced to reduce output by 60% after the peso's collapse made imported raw materials prohibitively expensive. The company operates a specialty chemicals plant in Buenos Aires province that supplies adhesives, coatings, and advanced materials to manufacturers across South America.

"The macroeconomic situation in Argentina has deteriorated to a point where normal business operations are no longer viable," Arkema CEO Thierry Le Hénaff told investors on an earnings call. "We are implementing force majeure on several supply contracts while we assess the situation."

Argentina's chemicals sector has contracted by 22% year-over-year, according to the Argentine Chemical and Petrochemical Industry Chamber (CIQyP). The crisis has been compounded by government-imposed currency controls that have restricted companies' ability to purchase foreign inputs and repatriate earnings.

Moody's downgraded Argentina's sovereign credit rating to Caa3, warning of further deterioration in the industrial base.
paraphrases:
BUENOS AIRES — Argentina's chemicals industry is in crisis as the country's economic collapse accelerates. French specialty chemicals maker Arkema SA disclosed that its Argentine operations have cut production by 60% after the peso's devaluation made imported raw materials unaffordable.

Arkema operates a plant in Buenos Aires province producing adhesives, coatings, and advanced materials for South American manufacturers. CEO Thierry Le Hénaff told investors that the company has declared force majeure on several regional supply contracts.

Argentina's chemicals sector has contracted 22% year-over-year according to the CIQyP industry chamber. Government-imposed currency controls have further restricted companies' ability to source foreign inputs. Moody's downgraded Argentina's sovereign rating to Caa3, warning of continued industrial deterioration.

BUENOS AIRES (AP) — Argentina's economic crisis has forced major chemicals manufacturers to scale back operations as hyperinflation and currency collapse make production untenable.

Arkema SA, the French specialty chemicals group, said its Argentine operations have reduced output by 60%. The company's Buenos Aires province plant, which supplies adhesives and advanced materials across South America, has declared force majeure on multiple supply agreements.

The Argentine Chemical and Petrochemical Industry Chamber reported a 22% contraction in the sector over the past year. Government currency controls have compounded the crisis by restricting access to foreign exchange needed for raw material imports. The peso's continued slide has made imported inputs prohibitively expensive for domestic manufacturers.

Deepening economic turmoil in Argentina is devastating the country's chemicals manufacturing base, with major international producers curtailing operations amid runaway inflation and currency instability.

Arkema SA reported that its Argentine subsidiary has been forced to cut output by 60% at its Buenos Aires province specialty chemicals plant. The facility produces adhesives, coatings, and advanced materials for customers throughout South America. Arkema's CEO described the macroeconomic environment as no longer viable for normal operations and confirmed the company has invoked force majeure clauses.

The CIQyP industry body reports a 22% year-over-year contraction in Argentina's chemicals sector. Moody's downgraded the country's sovereign rating to Caa3, signaling further risks for industrial companies operating in the market.
